metadata.summary: Extract multi-step business workflows from fat controllers into app/services/<BoundedContext>/<Action>Service classes with a single call method that returns ServiceResult (success/failure).

content.title: Rails Patterns (Service Objects)
content.summary: Rails service object pattern: extract multi-step business workflows from fat controllers/models into `app/services//Service` classes with a single `call` method that returns a `ServiceResult` (success/failure). Keeps controllers as pure HTTP adapters; business logic and transaction management live in services. This enforces a single responsibility, makes transaction boundaries explicit, and lets RSpec test business logic in isolation without loading the full HTTP stack.

why.p: Rails controllers balloon when they own database writes, mailer calls, audit logging, and job enqueuing. ActiveRecord callbacks create unmaintainable side-effect chains. Service objects enforce a single responsibility, make transaction boundaries explicit, and let RSpec test business logic in isolation without loading the full HTTP stack.

list.item: Controllers that have grown past ~100 lines and need workflow extraction
Multi-step business operations (signup, checkout, refund) that span multiple models in one transaction
Replacing fat-model callbacks when side effects have become unmanageable
Standardizing controller response shapes: every action maps `ServiceResult` → HTTP status
Trivial CRUD where a `before_action` + `model.save` already does the job — service objects add indirection without value
Pure data-access logic — that belongs in Query Objects or scopes, not services
One-off rake tasks or scripts — plain Ruby objects without the result wrapper are fine

rule.statement: Each service class has exactly one public method: call. If you need a second public method, extract a new service.
rule.rationale: A single call entry point makes the service's contract explicit and keeps RSpec tests focused on one responsibility.

rule.statement: Use keyword arguments in initialize (params:, current_user:). Avoid positional arguments.
rule.rationale: Keyword args are self-documenting, make mocking explicit, and prevent argument order mistakes when a service gains parameters.

rule.statement: Services must be free of HTTP/Rails concerns. They accept plain Ruby hashes or value objects; the controller maps ServiceResult to HTTP status codes.
rule.rationale: HTTP concerns in services prevent unit testing without loading Rails routing or request objects.

rule.statement: Namespace services by bounded context (Users::CreateService, Billing::RefundService), not by HTTP verb. Never name by REST action.
rule.rationale: Context namespacing scales to complex domains. HTTP-verb naming (PostsCreateService) couples the domain model to the HTTP layer.

rule.statement: Every service's call method returns a ServiceResult with success?, failure?, data, and errors. Never return raw ActiveRecord objects or raise domain exceptions directly to the controller.
rule.rationale: A single return type makes controller code uniform and prevents dual error paths (exception vs failure result) from diverging.

rule.statement: Wrap multi-step writes (save + mailer + job + audit) in ActiveRecord::Base.transaction. Lock the transaction to a single aggregate to avoid distributed-transaction bugs.
rule.rationale: Without a transaction, a mailer exception after user.save! leaves the user created but no email queued — silent inconsistency.

rule.statement: Catch ActiveRecord::RecordInvalid inside the transaction block, not outside. Return ServiceResult.failure with the validation errors.
rule.rationale: Catching outside the transaction after the rescue block may miss partial writes. RecordInvalid rolls back the transaction automatically.

example.title: Users::CreateService with transaction and deliver_later
example.code:
module Users class CreateService def initialize(params:, current_user: nil) @params = params @current_user = current_user end
def call user = User.new(user_params) ActiveRecord::Base.transaction do user.save! UserMailer.welcome(user).deliver_later AuditLog.create!( action: 'user.created', resource: user, actor: @current_user ) end ServiceResult.success(user) rescue ActiveRecord::RecordInvalid => e ServiceResult.failure(e.record.errors.full_messages) end
private
def user_params @params.slice(:name, :email, :password) end end end
example.note: Service accepts plain hash, not ActionController::Parameters. Mailer uses deliver_later. Transaction wraps save + audit.

example.title: Controller mapping ServiceResult to HTTP response
example.code:
module Api module V1 class UsersController < ApplicationController def create result = Users::CreateService.new( params: user_params, current_user: current_user ).call
if result.success? render json: UserSerializer.new(result.data), status: :created else render json: { errors: result.errors }, status: :unprocessable_entity end end
private
def user_params params.require(:user).permit(:name, :email, :password) end end end end
example.note: Controller only maps ServiceResult to HTTP shape. user_params is permitted before passing to service.

antipattern.description: Service has multiple public methods (call, create, update) or no call method at all.
antipattern.reason: Breaks single-responsibility; unclear which method is the entry point; harder to compose with organizers.

antipattern.description: rescue StandardError inside the service, swallowing all exceptions into ServiceResult.failure.
antipattern.reason: Hides real bugs (NoMethodError, TypeError) as domain failures. Only catch documented domain exceptions.

antipattern.description: Service returns raw ActiveRecord object on success, exception on failure — two divergent paths.
antipattern.reason: Controllers must handle both paths differently; ServiceResult enforces a uniform interface.

rule.statement: Reject services that reference params.require(...) or params.permit(...). The service accepts already-permitted plain hashes from the controller.
rule.rationale: ActionController::Parameters inside a service couples it to Rails and prevents unit testing.

rule.statement: Reject services that call deliver_now or perform_now for mailer/job dispatch. Use deliver_later / perform_later so service specs don't send real emails or run jobs synchronously.
rule.rationale: Synchronous dispatch in a service makes tests slow, side-effect-prone, and brittle.

template.purpose: ServiceResult value object with success/failure constructors
RSpec request + service spec template covering success, validation failure, DB rollback
